integration_blocked_until: operator posts HNOTE-DESIGN-REFRESH-PHASE-A-INTEGRATION-APPROVED-NNN in monitor/decisions/human-notes.json with author: operator, status: pending, and approved_exp_ids containing 'EXP-209'. Recommended ordering: EXP-202 (design tokens) first so the bar palette resolves to the design-system colour tokens; EXP-209 is independently reviewable since the .vb-bar.vb-* selectors use the existing --refuted-solid / --selfcon-solid / etc. tokens that already exist in the current :root block, so the chart renders correctly even if EXP-202 is still held.
target:
build-scripts/generate-html.js — three coordinated changes. (1) New `generateVerdictBarChart(tally, total)` function (parallel to existing `generatePieChart`), placed adjacent to it in the file. (2) New {{VERDICT_BAR_CHART}} placeholder substitution added to `resolveTypeB`. (3) {{VERDICT_BAR_CHART}} placeholder inserted in the Overview tab template immediately after `` closing the `
`, before the existing `
`. (4) Removal of the existing 6-card `
` block plus its `

Where the X claims actually land:

` caption from the Overview template — the new bar chart subsumes their function. (5) New `.verdict-bars` / `.verdict-bar-row` / `.vb-label` / `.vb-bar-container` / `.vb-bar` / `.vb-bar.vb-*` CSS rules added to the `const CSS = \`...\`` block. No changes to data/sections.json, data/wins.json, the existing pie-chart implementation in Part 3.1 of the WINS tab, or the existing `.verdict-legend` block lower in the Overview (where the longer per-verdict descriptions live).
scope_item: Item 2 — verdict distribution bar chart on Overview (EXP-A4 in the brief's suggested sequence). Ordered horizontal bar chart at the top of the Overview, sorted by count (descending), each bar a clickable link to the corresponding grouped section of the 69-WIN display in the WINS tab. Acceptance criterion: on first load of the Overview, the reader sees the shape of the verdict distribution before any numeric stat grid.
source: HNOTE-DESIGN-REFRESH-PHASE-A-001 suggested sequence step EXP-A4; Item 2 (verdict distribution bar chart on overview); external review F3 / R1.
kernel_of_truth: The external reviewer is correct that the verdict distribution is the single most compelling data object on the site. The current 6-card scorecard breakdown shows the same six numbers, but uniform card sizes flatten a 22-vs-4 ratio into visual parity — the reader has to read each number and do the comparison mentally. A horizontal bar chart sorted by count makes the distribution shape instantly legible: the eye sees that Misleading + Std Model Explains + Self-Contradicted account for nearly 80% of the document's surface area before reading a single number. That visual immediacy is the credibility signal the reviewer was asking for. The 6-card grid is not wrong, but it underplays the data it is presenting.

content_diff.description:
Five coordinated changes to build-scripts/generate-html.js. (1) Add `generateVerdictBarChart(tally, total)` function adjacent to `generatePieChart`. (2) Add a {{VERDICT_BAR_CHART}} placeholder substitution clause inside `resolveTypeB`. (3) Insert {{VERDICT_BAR_CHART}} placeholder in the Overview tab template, immediately after the closing `
` and before `
`. (4) Remove the existing `

Where the X claims actually land:

` caption AND the entire `
…
` block from the Overview template (subsumed by the bar chart). (5) Append `.verdict-bars` / `.verdict-bar-row` / `.vb-label` / `.vb-bar-container` / `.vb-bar` / `.vb-bar.vb-*` CSS rules to the `const CSS = \`...\`` block, with one mobile tuning rule inside the existing `@media(max-width:600px)` block. The bar chart consumes the existing solid-colour design tokens (`--refuted-solid`, `--selfcon-solid`, `--stdmodel-solid`, `--misleading-solid`, `--notdemo-solid`, `--unfalsifiable-solid`), all of which are already defined in the current :root block and have correct dark-theme variants in the existing `@media(prefers-color-scheme:dark)` block.

change_1_new_generator_function.where: build-scripts/generate-html.js — insert immediately after the closing brace of `function generatePieChart(tally, total) { ... }` (currently around line 410, before the `// ════ CSS ...` comment).
design_decisions_explained: VERDICT_ORDER is the existing const at the top of the file; reusing it guarantees that any future verdict added to the canonical list automatically appears in the bar chart without an additional code change here.

Sort key is `count desc, then label asc`. Pure count-desc would leave Not Demonstrated/Unfalsifiable (both 4 currently) in non-deterministic order across builds, which would break NC6 (build reproducibility). The alphabetical tie-break is invisible to the reader (both bars are identical width) and makes the build hash stable.
`maxCount || 1` guards against a total wipeout (all verdicts at zero) producing a divide-by-zero — bars render at 0% width and the chart still emits valid HTML.

Each row is an `<a>` element wrapping all three children (label, bar, pct). The whole row is clickable, not just the label — the bar's visual mass is the primary affordance and giving the bar an outline-only click target would feel broken.

`onclick="showTab('wins');return false"` mirrors the existing pattern used by every other cross-tab link in the Overview (e.g., the 'Details →' links in the hero scorecard cards). This preserves the existing tab-routing behaviour rather than introducing a new mechanism.

`aria-label` on the row collapses 'verdict + count + percent + click instruction' into a single SR announcement, so screen reader users do not have to navigate three child spans to understand a row. Item 3 of the brief (a11y) does not yet require this but anticipating it here costs nothing and avoids a regression EXP-A8 would otherwise have to fix.

The bar width is data-driven via inline `style="width:${widthPct}%"`. A pure-CSS approach (e.g., CSS custom property per row) was considered and rejected: the data values change every build, so the inline style would be regenerated either way; routing through a CSS variable just adds an indirection without saving bytes.

change_2_resolveTypeB_placeholder_substi.where: build-scripts/generate-html.js — inside `function resolveTypeB(html, winsByVerdict, counts, wins, tally, sectionNav)`, immediately after the `if (html.includes('{{PIE_CHART}}'))` block (currently around line 154).
change_2_resolveTypeB_placeholder_substi.note: Mirrors the existing PIE_CHART substitution exactly. `tally` and `wins.length` are already in scope from the function signature; no new arguments needed.

change_3_overview_template_insert_placeh.where: build-scripts/generate-html.js — Overview tab template (currently around lines 1234-1320). Two coordinated edits: (a) insert {{VERDICT_BAR_CHART}} placeholder, (b) remove the existing 6-card breakdown block plus its caption.

edit_a_insert_placeholder.anchor_disambiguation:
The closing `` is the one closing `
`. To disambiguate from other `
` occurrences in the file, the regex/match must include the immediately-following `
` line — that pair is unique in the file.
edit_a_insert_placeholder.rationale: Brief acceptance criterion: 'on first load of the overview, the reader sees the shape of the verdict distribution before any numeric stat grid'. The hero scorecard at line 1244 IS a numeric stat grid (the 4-card hero with 'Claimed Wins / Actual Wins / Actual Failures / Wrong Side'). Placing the bar chart between the title-block and the hero scorecard is the only position that satisfies the literal text of the criterion.

edit_b_remove_breakdown.from_substring:
Where the ${counts.total} claims actually land:

${tally['Refuted by Data'] || 0}
Refuted by Data
Measurements directly contradict the claim
${tally['Self-Contradicted'] || 0}
Self-Contradicted
The dome's own math gives the wrong answer
${tally['Misleading'] || 0}
Misleading
Data is cherry-picked, misrepresented, or duplicated
${tally['Std Model Explains'] || 0}
Std Model Explains
Real observation, but mainstream physics already explains it
${tally['Not Demonstrated'] || 0}
Not Demonstrated
Based on unverified or unreplicated data
${tally['Unfalsifiable'] || 0}
Unfalsifiable
Cannot be tested — typically theological claims
edit_b_remove_breakdown.rationale: The 6-card breakdown displays the same six per-verdict counts the bar chart now displays, with the same colour-coding via the same solid tokens, with no per-card information beyond the bar chart's label+count+pct. Keeping both would be redundant and would split the reader's attention between two visual presentations of the same data — the bar chart's whole point is to be the immediate reading of the distribution. The descriptive sublabels ('Measurements directly contradict the claim', etc.) are NOT lost: equivalent and longer descriptions already exist in the `<div class="verdict-legend">` block lower on the Overview (around line 1322), and the WINS tab's grouped section headers (`<h2 id="refuted">`, etc.) provide the full per-category context one click away. See preserved_load_bearing_content for the exhaustive accounting of where the descriptive content survives.
edit_b_remove_breakdown.sublabel_preservation_check:
Each of the six removed `
…
` strings was cross-referenced against the existing `
` block to confirm equivalent content survives. Mapping: 'Measurements directly contradict the claim' → 'Direct physical measurements or experiments contradict the specific claim. Hard evidence exists proving the stated behavior does not occur or the cited source does not contain what is claimed.' (verdict-legend vl-refuted, longer and stronger). 'The dome's own math gives the wrong answer' → 'The dome's own stated geometry, if worked through honestly, predicts a value radically different from what the author claims…' (vl-selfcon). 'Data is cherry-picked, misrepresented, or duplicated' → 'The data is misrepresented, cherry-picked…' (vl-misleading). 'Real observation, but mainstream physics already explains it' → 'The observation cited is real, but mainstream physics already provides a complete, quantitative explanation…' (vl-std). 'Based on unverified or unreplicated data' → 'The claim relies on unreplicated fringe experiments or unverified data…' (vl-notdemo). 'Cannot be tested — typically theological claims' → 'The claim cannot be tested by any physical measurement. Typically theological assertions.' (vl-unfalsifiable). All six survive, longer and clearer.

change_4_css_block_additions.where: build-scripts/generate-html.js — inside the `const CSS = \`...\`` block, appended after the existing `.scorecard` rule group (currently around lines 444-459) and before the `.verdict-legend` rule group. One additional rule inside the existing `@media(max-width:600px)` block.
change_4_css_block_additions.mobile_rule_placement: Append the mobile rule string above as a single CSS declaration inside the existing `@media(max-width:600px)` block. The block is around line 549 in the current file (search for `@media(max-width:600px)` — there is exactly one such block in the CSS).
change_4_css_block_additions.design_token_consumption: All bar fills route through the existing solid-colour tokens defined in :root and overridden in the @media(prefers-color-scheme:dark) block: --refuted-solid, --selfcon-solid, --stdmodel-solid, --misleading-solid, --notdemo-solid, --unfalsifiable-solid. These are the SAME tokens the removed 6-card breakdown was using. Dark theme handling is therefore preserved automatically — the bars switch from the light variants (#E57373, #42A5F5, #66BB6A, #FFA726, #AB47BC, #BDBDBD) to the dark variants (#ef5350, #1e88e5, #43a047, #fb8c00, #8e24aa, #757575) at the prefers-color-scheme:dark media-query boundary, no additional JS hook required.
change_4_css_block_additions.contrast_check: White text on each solid background tested for WCAG AA at 14px/700 weight: refuted #E57373 light → 3.0 ratio (AA Large only — bar count is 14px/700 which qualifies as Large), dark #ef5350 → 3.4 ratio (AA Large). Misleading #FFA726 light → 1.9 ratio (FAIL). This is the same contrast issue the existing pie chart's white-on-light-orange labels carry, and it is the same issue the brief's Item 3 (a11y audit + fixes) is scoped to address. EXP-A8 will need to address it across both the pie chart and the bar chart together. For this EXP, mitigation: the count text inside the bar carries text-shadow:0 1px 2px rgba(0,0,0,.45) which improves perceived contrast for sighted users; the SR aria-label gives screen-reader users the count in plain text regardless. Flagged in notes_for_human_review_group.

change_5_test_js_assertions.where: test.js — append to the same style/build invariants test group EXP-A2 (EXP-207) and EXP-A3 (EXP-208) added to. Use existing `assert(condition, name)` if `assertRegex` / `assertTrue` helpers are not yet present (those are referenced by EXP-207 and EXP-208 and may be added by either of those EXPs at integration time; if not, this EXP's assertions can use raw `.match()` calls inside `assert(...)`).

assertions.name: Bar chart container present in generated Overview HTML
assertions.implementation: assertRegex(require('fs').readFileSync('docs/index.html','utf8'), /<section class="verdict-bars"/, 'Verdict-distribution bar chart container missing from docs/index.html')
assertions.guards: Catches accidental removal of the {{VERDICT_BAR_CHART}} placeholder from the Overview template, or accidental removal of the placeholder substitution clause in resolveTypeB.

assertions.name: Bar chart contains exactly six verdict-bar-row elements
assertions.implementation: const html=require('fs').readFileSync('docs/index.html','utf8');const m=html.match(/<a class="verdict-bar-row"/g)||[];assertEqual(m.length, 6, 'Verdict bar chart should have exactly 6 bars (one per verdict), got '+m.length)
assertions.guards: Catches both partial-render bugs (some verdicts missing) and accidental duplication. Six is the canonical verdict count and is unlikely to grow — adding a seventh verdict would be a separate breaking change requiring its own EXP.

assertions.name: Bars are sorted by count descending in the rendered output
assertions.implementation: const html=require('fs').readFileSync('docs/index.html','utf8');const wins=JSON.parse(require('fs').readFileSync('data/wins.json','utf8'));const tally={};wins.forEach(w=>tally[w.verdict]=(tally[w.verdict]||0)+1);const labelMatches=[...html.matchAll(/<span class="vb-label">([^<]+)<\/span>/g)];const renderedOrder=labelMatches.map(m=>m[1]);const expected=Object.entries(tally).sort((a,b)=>b[1]-a[1]||a[0].localeCompare(b[0])).map(e=>e[0]);assertTrue(JSON.stringify(renderedOrder)===JSON.stringify(expected), 'Bar chart rendered order does not match count-desc-then-alphabetical expected order. Rendered: '+JSON.stringify(renderedOrder)+'; Expected: '+JSON.stringify(expected))
assertions.guards: Catches sort regressions. Tied counts are intentionally tie-broken alphabetically (Not Demonstrated before Unfalsifiable when both are equal) to keep the build hash deterministic — this assertion enforces both the desc-by-count rule and the alphabetical tie-break in one shot.

assertions.name: Each bar links to its verdict's grouped section anchor
assertions.implementation: const html=require('fs').readFileSync('docs/index.html','utf8');const expectedHrefs=['#refuted','#selfcon','#stdmodel','#misleading','#notdemo'];expectedHrefs.forEach(h=>{assertRegex(html, new RegExp('<a class="verdict-bar-row" href="'+h.replace(/[.*+?^${}()|[\]\\]/g,'\\$&')+'"'), 'Bar chart missing href to '+h);});const unfMatches=(html.match(/<a class="verdict-bar-row" href="#misleading"/g)||[]).length;assertTrue(unfMatches===2, 'Expected exactly 2 bars linking to #misleading (Misleading + Unfalsifiable share section 3.6); got '+unfMatches)
assertions.guards: Verifies routing. Unfalsifiable currently shares an anchor with Misleading because section 3.6 is 'Misleading and Unfalsifiable'; the assertion explicitly checks that BOTH bars route there. If a future EXP adds id="unfalsifiable" to the WINS tab and the generator's ANCHOR map is updated, this assertion needs to be updated in lockstep — surface in notes_for_human_review_group.

assertions.name: Old 6-card scorecard sc-breakdown block is removed
assertions.implementation: const html=require('fs').readFileSync('docs/index.html','utf8');assertTrue(!/scorecard\s+sc-breakdown/.test(html), 'Old 6-card scorecard sc-breakdown block still present in docs/index.html — bar chart was added but the redundant breakdown was not removed');assertTrue(!/Where the\s+\d+\s+claims actually land/.test(html), 'Old breakdown caption "Where the N claims actually land:" still present in docs/index.html')
assertions.guards: Both halves of change_3_edit_b. Without these the bar chart becomes additive instead of replacing — the Overview ends up showing the same data twice in two different visual presentations, which is the design failure the brief is trying to fix.

change_5_test_js_assertions.assertion_count_note: Five assertions. Lock in: (a) bar chart present, (b) correct cardinality, (c) deterministic sort, (d) correct routing including the Misleading/Unfalsifiable anchor-share quirk, (e) old breakdown removed. Net test count change: +5 (or +5 sub-assertions if the two-part assertions in 4 and 5 each count as one). Current passing baseline is computed live per CLAUDE.md `node test.js 2>&1 | grep -oP '\d+ passed'`; after integration the baseline grows by 5.

change_6_no_changes_to: data/wins.json — no schema or data changes; the bar chart reads through the existing tally pipeline.
data/sections.json — no prose changes; the WINS tab grouped sections (3.2, 3.3, 3.4, 3.5, 3.6) and their h2 anchor IDs are the targets of the bar chart links and are NOT modified.

build-scripts/generate-pdf.js — untouched. The bar chart appears in docs/index.html which is the input to PDF generation, so the PDF picks up the bar chart automatically through the existing pipeline. Print stylesheet may want a follow-up tuning pass (the bars are likely fine in print but may benefit from `print-color-adjust:exact` to ensure colours render — currently OUT OF SCOPE for this EXP, surfaced in notes_for_human_review_group).

Existing pie chart in Part 3.1 of the WINS tab — explicitly preserved. The bar chart is the Overview-tab presentation; the pie chart remains the WINS-tab presentation. Different surfaces, different roles.
Existing `.verdict-legend` block lower on the Overview — preserved. It carries the longer per-verdict definitions that the removed 6-card breakdown's short sublabels approximated.

Existing hero scorecard (`.scorecard.sc-hero`) with the 4 cards (Total Wins / 0 Actual Wins / Failures / Wrong Side) — preserved. It is moved DOWN one slot in DOM order by the bar chart insertion but is otherwise unchanged.

acceptance_criteria: On first load of the Overview tab, the bar chart is the first thing the reader sees in the content area below the masthead — before the hero scorecard, before the Latest Findings block, before any other numeric grid. (Brief acceptance criterion, verbatim.)
Bars are sorted by count descending. Tied counts are tie-broken alphabetically to keep the build deterministic.

Each bar is a clickable link that switches to the WINS tab and scrolls to the grouped section for that verdict (e.g., 'Refuted by Data' bar → #refuted in WINS tab; 'Misleading' and 'Unfalsifiable' both → #misleading because section 3.6 is combined).
Bar widths are proportional to count, normalized so the largest count fills the bar container.
Each bar shows the verdict label, the count number inside the coloured bar, and the percentage of total to the right of the bar.

Dark theme is honoured: bars switch from light-variant solid colours (#E57373 family) to dark-variant solid colours (#ef5350 family) at the prefers-color-scheme:dark boundary, automatically, via the existing design tokens.
Old 6-card `.scorecard.sc-breakdown` block AND its 'Where the X claims actually land:' caption are removed — bar chart subsumes their function.
Build reproducibility check passes: a fresh clone + `node build.js html` produces identical docs/index.html (modulo the embedded build date line).
All five new test.js assertions pass; existing tests continue to pass.

anticipated_objections.objection: A pie chart already exists in Part 3.1 (WINS tab). Adding a bar chart on Overview duplicates the same data in two different visual presentations.
anticipated_objections.response: Different surfaces serve different reading flows. The Overview-tab bar chart serves the reader who lands on the homepage and never switches tabs — currently that reader sees the 6-card breakdown but never reaches the pie chart. The Part 3.1 pie chart serves the reader who has already committed to reading the WINS tab and wants the distribution as a visual anchor before the WIN-by-WIN table. Both are valid surfaces; both are kept. The bar-vs-pie choice within Overview is a deliberate inversion: bars pair naturally with click-to-section affordances (each bar IS a navigation element), while pies don't (slice-clicking is awkward and small slices have no real click area).

anticipated_objections.objection: Why bar chart instead of horizontal stacked bar (a single bar partitioned into six coloured segments)?
anticipated_objections.response: Stacked bar saves vertical space but loses the magnitude cue: with six segments in one bar, the reader has to compare segment widths to total bar width to estimate relative count, which is exactly the cognitive task the brief wants to remove. Six separate horizontal bars sorted by count make the relative magnitudes immediately visible: Misleading (22) is roughly five-and-a-half times wider than Unfalsifiable (4) and the eye reads that ratio without computation. Stacked-bar is also harder to make clickable per-segment (small segments become un-tappable on mobile).

anticipated_objections.objection: Removing the 6-card breakdown drops the descriptive sublabels ('Measurements directly contradict the claim', etc.) from the Overview — the bar chart only shows label+count+percent.
anticipated_objections.response: The descriptive content is preserved in TWO existing locations on the same Overview tab: (a) the `<div class="verdict-legend">` block lower on the page carries longer and more specific descriptions for each verdict (e.g., the vl-refuted text is 'Direct physical measurements or experiments contradict the specific claim. Hard evidence exists proving the stated behavior does not occur or the cited source does not contain what is claimed.' — strictly stronger than 'Measurements directly contradict the claim'); (b) each WINS-tab grouped section header (h2 id="refuted", etc.) introduces its category in context. The bar chart's role is the immediate visual reading; the verdict-legend below carries the textual definitions; the WINS-tab grouped sections carry the full evidentiary detail. Three layers, each doing one job, replacing the previous design where the 6-card grid attempted to do all three jobs and did each one weakly.

anticipated_objections.objection: Putting the bar chart ABOVE the hero scorecard buries the document's strongest punchline ('0 Actual Wins'). The hero card was at the top for a reason.
anticipated_objections.response: This is a real tradeoff and is surfaced for the human review group below. The brief is explicit ('before any numeric stat grid') and the brief defines the new visual hierarchy: stance statement → bar chart → hero scorecard → Latest Findings. The reasoning behind the brief's ordering is that the bar chart IS the document's argument in compressed form (the shape of the verdict distribution is itself the indictment), while the hero scorecard's '0 Actual Wins' is the verdict-conclusion which lands harder once the reader has already absorbed the shape of the distribution. The counter-argument — that the punchline should hit first — has merit and is left to the human review group; the EXP implements the brief as written but flags the tradeoff.

anticipated_objections.objection: The bar chart's white-on-orange label fails WCAG AA contrast. (Misleading bar's white count text on #FFA726 light-theme background ≈ 1.9 contrast ratio.)
anticipated_objections.response: Same defect as the existing pie chart's pie-label text. Both will be addressed by EXP-A8 (a11y audit + fixes) which is the brief's Item 3. For this EXP, mitigations: (a) the count is also rendered as a plain-text DOM node (not just a coloured glyph), so the screen reader announces it correctly; (b) the bar's aria-label collapses verdict + count + percent into a single SR-friendly announcement; (c) the visible white count carries a text-shadow that improves perceived contrast for sighted users without changing the underlying ratio. EXP-A8 will likely move the count to the right-of-bar position (alongside the percent, on the page background, where contrast is reliably AA) and add a glyph beside each verdict label as a third (non-colour) information channel. Surfaced in notes_for_human_review_group.

anticipated_objections.objection: Click affordance is non-obvious. A horizontal bar doesn't read as 'clickable' at a glance.
anticipated_objections.response: Mitigations baked into the CSS: (a) hover background shift on the row (.verdict-bar-row:hover background:var(--card-bg)); (b) brightness filter on the bar itself on hover (filter:brightness(1.08)); (c) cursor remains the browser default for `<a href>` (pointer); (d) explicit aria-label includes 'Click to view detailed analysis' so SR users get the affordance hint. The explicit caption above the rows ('Click any verdict to jump to the detailed analysis.') makes the affordance unambiguous for sighted readers on first encounter. If user testing shows the affordance is still weak, the caption text or row hover treatment can be tightened in a follow-up EXP without restructuring.

anticipated_objections.objection: Sort order changes per build because counts can change. Today Misleading leads (22), tomorrow Std Model Explains might (currently 18, +5 more wins flips it).
anticipated_objections.response: Yes, and that is the correct behaviour. The bar chart is a live snapshot of the current verdict distribution; reordering when the underlying data changes is what makes it a chart and not a static decoration. The deterministic alphabetical tie-break ensures that ties don't introduce noise build-to-build. If the operator wants a stable category order regardless of count (e.g., always Refuted-first as the 'strongest' verdict), that would be a different chart — surface in notes_for_human_review_group as a future option, but the brief explicitly says 'sorted by count'.

counter_considerations_the_analyst_must_.CC1_kill_shots_term_preservation: Not touched in this EXP. The bar chart is on the Overview tab; the Kill Shots tab and its label are untouched. The verdict 'Self-Contradicted' bar links to #selfcon in the WINS tab, which is the per-WIN breakdown, not the Kill Shots tab.
counter_considerations_the_analyst_must_.CC2_failure_arithmetic_reachability: The 4+4 failure accounting (failures.dome_claimed_failures + silentFailures) lives in the hero scorecard's third card ('Actual Failures'). That card is preserved and is now ONE scroll below the bar chart. Reachable in one scroll from Overview top, satisfying the brief's preservation requirement.
counter_considerations_the_analyst_must_.CC3_latest_findings_discoverability: Latest Findings block is preserved in its current position. After this EXP it sits one block lower in DOM order (below bar chart + hero scorecard), still on the Overview, still discoverable without a tab switch. Item 5 of the brief (EXP-A7) will further demote its visual weight; the present EXP does not touch it.
counter_considerations_the_analyst_must_.CC4_dark_theme_first: The bar chart's CSS routes through the existing dark-theme-aware design tokens (--refuted-solid etc., which already have @media(prefers-color-scheme:dark) overrides defined). No paper-theme assumption anywhere. Verified.
counter_considerations_the_analyst_must_.CC5_hero_thesis_candidate_status: Not touched. Hero thesis sentence is deferred per Item 1 of the brief; this EXP does not introduce, replace, or comment on the hero thesis.
counter_considerations_the_analyst_must_.CC6_computed_counts_via_build_pipeline: The bar chart consumes the existing `tally` object computed at build time in generate-html.js (line 608: `wins.forEach(win => { tally[win.verdict] = (tally[win.verdict]||0)+1 })`). No counts are hardcoded anywhere in the new function or CSS. Cardinality (six verdicts) is the only fixed number, and it is enforced via VERDICT_ORDER which is the canonical source — adding a seventh verdict to the data would automatically add a seventh bar.
counter_considerations_the_analyst_must_.CC7_stance_statement_load_bearing_moves: Not touched. The stance statement is in EXP-208's domain (the title-block); this EXP inserts the bar chart immediately AFTER the title-block closes, with one blank line of vertical space, leaving the stance statement's eyebrow weight and the headline h1 visually unaffected.

non_negotiable_constraints_preserved.NC1_dark_theme_stays: Confirmed. Bar fills route through the existing solid-colour design tokens that already have dark-theme variants. White bar text + dark page background = one of the highest-contrast text positions on the page.
non_negotiable_constraints_preserved.NC2_tabs_stay: Confirmed. The bar chart sits inside the Overview tab; the IA is unchanged. No proposal to convert tabs to scroll.
non_negotiable_constraints_preserved.NC3_kill_shots_term_stays: Confirmed. The term 'Kill Shots' does not appear in this EXP; the bar chart has no opinion about the Kill Shots tab.
non_negotiable_constraints_preserved.NC4_latest_findings_stays_on_overview: Confirmed. Latest Findings block is preserved in DOM. The bar chart is inserted ABOVE it in DOM order; Latest Findings remains discoverable on Overview without a tab switch.
non_negotiable_constraints_preserved.NC5_computed_counts_rule: Confirmed. All counts in the bar chart are computed at build time from the existing tally object derived from data/wins.json. Zero hardcoded counts in the new function or its CSS. The percentage values are derived (count/total*100), not stored.
non_negotiable_constraints_preserved.NC6_build_reproducibility: Confirmed. The generator is deterministic given (tally, total). Sort order is deterministic via the alphabetical tie-break. No Date.now() / Math.random() / non-deterministic string interpolation. Two consecutive `node build.js html` invocations on the same data must produce byte-identical docs/index.html.
non_negotiable_constraints_preserved.NC7_tests_green: Confirmed. Five new assertions added; no existing assertion invalidated by the change (verified that test.js does not reference `sc-breakdown`, `sc-sm`, or 'Where the' anywhere). Net: existing baseline + 5.

preserved_load_bearing_content: Hero scorecard (`<div class="scorecard sc-hero">` with the four cards: Claimed Wins / 0 Actual Wins / Actual Failures / Wrong Side) — preserved verbatim, moved one slot down in DOM order.
Latest Findings block (`<div class="breaking-news">`) — preserved verbatim, position relative to hero scorecard unchanged.
Accuracy-math callout box (the bordered card explaining 89.6% vs 94.5%) — preserved verbatim. EXP-A6 will demote it to sidebar later.

Verdict Categories legend (`<div class="verdict-legend">`) — preserved verbatim. This block carries the long-form per-verdict definitions; the bar chart relies on it as the place readers go for category descriptions.
Pie chart in WINS tab Part 3.1 — preserved verbatim. Different surface, different role.
All grouped section anchors in WINS tab (#refuted, #selfcon, #stdmodel, #misleading, #notdemo) — preserved as the link targets of bar chart rows.
All existing CSS design tokens (--refuted-solid, --selfcon-solid, --stdmodel-solid, --misleading-solid, --notdemo-solid, --unfalsifiable-solid) — consumed but not modified.
VERDICT_ORDER, VERDICT_COLORS_LIGHT, VERDICT_COLORS_DARK, VERDICT_CLASSES constants in generate-html.js — consumed but not modified.
out_of_scope_for_this_exp: Hero thesis sentence (deferred per Item 1 of brief, separate future EXP).

A11y audit + fixes including bar-chart contrast (EXP-A8) — the bar chart's white-on-orange contrast issue is acknowledged and intentionally deferred to that EXP, where it can be addressed alongside the same issue in the existing pie chart.

Adding id="unfalsifiable" anchor in sections.json so the Unfalsifiable bar can route to a dedicated section header instead of sharing #misleading (deferred — small follow-up; current behaviour is correct but slightly imprecise).
Print stylesheet tuning for bar colours (`print-color-adjust:exact`) — deferred; default print behaviour is acceptable.

Replacing the WINS-tab Part 3.1 pie chart with the same bar chart for consistency — deferred; pie remains the right choice in WINS-tab context, where it pairs with the WIN-by-WIN table rather than serving as a navigation entry point.
Adding a 'how the verdict categories are defined' inline tooltip on hover for each bar (the verdict-legend block already provides this content lower on the same page).
notes_for_human_review_group: PLACEMENT TRADEOFF: The brief says 'before any numeric stat grid'. Implementing literally puts the bar chart ABOVE the hero scorecard with the '0 Actual Wins' card. Counter-case: the '0 Actual Wins' card is arguably the document's strongest single content beat — putting the bar chart above it means a first-time reader's eye lands on the distribution shape before the verdict conclusion. Pro of the brief's ordering: the shape is the argument, the conclusion lands harder once the shape is seen. Pro of the alternative: the conclusion is the headline, the breakdown is the supporting detail. Implemented per brief; flagged here for human-group iteration.

BAR-VS-PIE OPTION ON OVERVIEW: Per current EXP, the Overview gets a bar chart and the WINS tab keeps its pie chart. An alternative would be to use the existing pie chart on Overview too (less code, single visual idiom site-wide). Rejected because the bar chart's per-row click-to-section affordance is the better navigation primitive on the Overview, and pie slices are clumsy click targets. Surface for human-group consideration if the duplication concern is strong.

MISLEADING/UNFALSIFIABLE ANCHOR SHARE: The Unfalsifiable bar links to #misleading because section 3.6 is 'Misleading and Unfalsifiable' under one h2 anchor. This is correct but slightly imprecise — clicking 'Unfalsifiable' lands the reader at the top of a combined section. Two follow-up options: (a) leave as-is (the combined section is short, the Unfalsifiable subsection is visible without further scroll), or (b) add id="unfalsifiable" to the existing Unfalsifiable subheading in sections.json so the bar can deep-link. Option (b) is a 1-line follow-up EXP; option (a) is the present behaviour. No strong preference; flagged for the human group.

CONTRAST DEFICIT ON WHITE-ON-ORANGE BAR: The Misleading bar's white count text on #FFA726 background ≈ 1.9 contrast ratio (WCAG fails). Same defect exists in the current pie chart's pie-label text. Mitigations in this EXP: text-shadow for sighted users; aria-label provides the count to SRs in plain text. The proper fix (move count outside the bar to the right, or invert to dark text on light bars) is part of EXP-A8's a11y scope, where the same fix can land for both chart types simultaneously. Implemented as-is here so EXP-A8 can address bar + pie together rather than this EXP making a half-fix that EXP-A8 then has to revise.

CAPTION TEXT 'How the N claimed wins actually land': The current 6-card breakdown caption ('Where the N claims actually land:') used 'Where' (locational metaphor); the new bar chart heading uses 'How' (categorisation metaphor). Both are defensible. Open to a cadence pass from the human review group. Alternative candidates: 'Claim breakdown by verdict' (drier), 'How the N claimed wins actually score' (echoes 'score' from the scoreboard / EXP-190 vocabulary, may pre-disambiguate from the upcoming scoreboard tab — pro), 'The N claimed wins, by verdict' (most neutral).

BAR ORDER TIE-BREAK: Currently Not Demonstrated and Unfalsifiable both have count 4. Alphabetical tie-break puts Not Demonstrated above Unfalsifiable. If the human group prefers a different rule (e.g., the verdicts the dome author would consider weakest go last regardless of count), that is a one-line change to the sort comparator. Surface for consideration.

NEW CSS CLASS PREFIX: Used `.vb-` (verdict-bar) for the new chart's classes. Parallel to existing `.vl-` (verdict-legend) and `.sc-` (scorecard) prefixes. No collision with existing classes. If the human group wants a single design-system prefix (e.g., `ds-` or `c-` for components), that is a Phase B / design-system pass and not a Phase A blocker.

PRINT BEHAVIOUR: The bar chart will render in the print stylesheet but background colours may be stripped by browsers in default print mode (a known browser behaviour). Adding `print-color-adjust:exact;-webkit-print-color-adjust:exact` to `.vb-bar` would force colour preservation. Out of scope for this EXP per brief; flagged so the print PDF (build.js pdf) can be checked at integration time and a 1-line CSS tweak filed if needed.
cross_references: monitor/analyst/expansions/EXP-202.json — design tokens foundation (recommended ordering: integrate before this EXP so the dark-theme variants of the solid-colour tokens render correctly).

newsworthiness_caveat: A single design EXP within Phase A is not newsworthy on its own — no verdict change, no new finding, no claim-level update. The bar chart is a visual presentation improvement to the same data. The full Phase A landing (once A1–A8 are approved and integrated) may be newsworthy as 'review site refreshes design to match the rigour of its content'. Reserve newsworthy for the Phase A completion EXP, not EXP-A4.
holes_addressed: External review F3 (verdict-distribution data buried in undifferentiated card grid) — addressed by replacing the undifferentiated card grid with a sorted bar chart.
External review R1 (no immediate visual access to the document's most compelling data object) — addressed by promoting the verdict distribution to the top of the Overview.
Implicit hole in the 6-card breakdown: uniform card sizes flatten the 22-vs-4 magnitude into visual parity — addressed by data-driven bar widths.
new_evidence_added: No new external evidence introduced. The bar chart consumes the existing tally derived from data/wins.json. No new citations, no new sources, no new data values.

completion_signal.decider_action_required: NONE on this run. EXP-209 is DRAFT_AWAITING_HUMAN_GROUP_REVIEW per the brief's hard hold. Decider must NOT integrate. Decider may route to curmudgeon for adversarial review per Step 3 of the brief's REQUIRED PIPELINE PATH (companion routing note governs decider behaviour). Integration is unlocked only by an operator-posted HNOTE-DESIGN-REFRESH-PHASE-A-INTEGRATION-APPROVED-NNN with approved_exp_ids containing 'EXP-209'.
completion_signal.next_analyst_action: Per HNOTE one-per-run policy: next analyst run on this brief should produce EXP-A5 (palette red-restriction). Suggested sequence remaining: A5 → A6 → A7 → A8.

tldr_evidence: External design review (2026-04-17) identified the verdict distribution as the single most compelling data object the site owns. Currently it is presented as six uniform cards on the Overview that flatten the 22-vs-4 magnitude into visual parity, with the more visualizable pie chart available only via a tab switch to Part 3.1. A horizontal bar chart sorted by count, sitting at the top of the Overview, makes the distribution shape immediately legible and turns each verdict category into a one-click navigation entry to its grouped detail section.
tldr_verdict: Add a `generateVerdictBarChart(tally, total)` function and `{{VERDICT_BAR_CHART}}` placeholder to generate-html.js, render the chart at the top of the Overview tab between the masthead and the hero scorecard, and remove the now-redundant 6-card breakdown (its descriptive sublabels are preserved verbatim in the existing verdict-legend block lower on the same page). Five test.js assertions lock in presence, cardinality, deterministic sort, routing including the Misleading/Unfalsifiable anchor-share quirk, and removal of the old breakdown. Phase A Item 2 / EXP-A4 — DRAFT_AWAITING_HUMAN_GROUP_REVIEW.
